primer.markdown:
# The handshake that decides what each side can say

Nothing useful travels over MCP until both ends have agreed on what each of them speaks. That agreement happens in the very first message pair — the `initialize` handshake — and the word to watch for is **capabilities**.

A client opens the transport, sends an `initialize` request advertising its protocol version and its own capability bag, and waits. The server answers with its matching capability bag. Only the capabilities that appear in *both* bags are valid for the rest of the session. `tools`, `resources`, `prompts`, `logging`, `sampling` — each is a separate switch, and the switches are set once at the top of the conversation.

The `mcp-categorical` skill makes this concrete in the simplest possible TypeScript server. Look at the `new Server(...)` line: the second argument is a literal capability declaration, `{ capabilities: { tools: {} } }`. That object is the payload the server ships back during `initialize`. Writing `{ tools: {} }` tells the client: _I speak tools; I do not speak resources or prompts._ If the client later sends a `resources/list` request, the server is free (and correct) to reject it.

That means your first architectural decision as a server author is not _which_ tool to register. It is _which primitive families_ you will claim in this capability object. The handshake is a cut — every promise your server will keep, and every promise it will refuse to keep, is declared in that one literal.
primer.key_insight: `initialize` is not a greeting. It is a cut. The `capabilities` object is a contract the server signs, once, before any real request arrives.

visual.alt: A handshake sequence diagram between a Client column on the left and a Server column on the right. The client sends an `initialize` request carrying a `capabilities: { sampling: {}, roots: {} }` bag. The server answers with an `initialize` response carrying `capabilities: { tools: {}, resources: {} }`. A dashed box in the centre labelled `intersection` highlights the keys present in both bags — this is the agreed vocabulary for the rest of the session.

sandbox_policy.csp: default-src 'none'; style-src 'unsafe-inline'; script-src 'unsafe-inline'; img-src data:; font-src https://fonts.gstatic.com; connect-src 'none'; frame-ancestors 'self'

interactive.success_criterion: Learner toggles the server `capabilities` switches (`tools`, `resources`, `prompts`) and then issues a simulated `tools/list` request. When `tools` is off, the simulator returns a `method not found` error; when `tools` is on, it returns an empty tool array. The success pill appears once the learner has seen both states and confirmed that the rejection is a direct consequence of the `initialize` declaration.

questions.prompt: In the `mcp-categorical` TypeScript example, which literal declares the server's capabilities to the client?
questions.explanation: `skills/integrations/mcp-categorical/SKILL.md` line 33: `{ capabilities: { tools: {} } }` — the second argument to `new Server(...)`. That object is what the server ships back in its `initialize` response, and it is the only place a capability declaration appears in this example.

questions.prompt: A server ships `{ capabilities: { tools: {} } }` at `initialize`. The client then sends a `resources/list` request. What is the correct behaviour?
questions.explanation: The server has not declared `resources` in its capability bag, so the client asked for a method the contract does not cover. The server should respond with a JSON-RPC `method not found` (or equivalent capability-mismatch) error. Capability negotiation is the gate — non-declared methods are not handled.

questions.prompt: Why is `capabilities` negotiated at `initialize` rather than per-request?
questions.explanation: Per-request negotiation would force every call to carry metadata about which primitives are available, and both ends would have to re-agree every time. A one-shot handshake establishes the shared vocabulary once; every subsequent frame is just a method call inside that agreed contract.
